english: Aiyu with grass jelly and pearls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for aiyu jelly; mapped to the nearest parent class 'jelly food product' (FOODON_03301710). Aiyu jelly is a fig-seed-derived gel dessert with assorted toppings.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as the closest defensible category for plant-based jelly desserts. No specific aiyu jelly entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for aiyu jelly or fig-seed jelly dessert as of 2024.

main_ingredients: fresh fruit (longan, lychee, or other seasonal fruit)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of aiyu jelly with toppings is approximately 300 g, comprising the aiyu jelly base, assorted toppings such as grass jelly, tapioca pearls, or fresh fruit, and sugar syrup or lemon juice.

nutrient_source.title: Aiyu Jelly with Toppings — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Aiyu Jelly with Toppings (cr_id F02030002). Saturated fat estimated from known coconut-based topping composition as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; estimation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate using sweetened jelly dessert / tapioca pearl composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for aiyu jelly with toppings exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low-medium boundary) based on: (1) aiyu jelly base is a low-GI pectin-type gel (estimated GI ~30–40); (2) tapioca pearls have a published GI of approximately 56–70 (University of Sydney GI Database, tapioca); (3) sugar syrup raises the composite GI; (4) weighted composite estimate yields approximately 50. GL per serving calculated as GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 50 × (10.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 50 × 31.5 / 100 = 15.75.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.2 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g) and estimated saturated fat ~5.8 g/100 g from coconut-based toppings
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request no added sugar syrup and avoid coconut-based toppings, as the combination of high sugar and saturated fat can impair vascular function and elevate blood pressure despite the low sodium content of 9 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 9.2 g/100 g with low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g) and a composite GI of approximately 50 driven by sugar syrup and tapioca pearl toppings
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume aiyu jelly with toppings only in small portions, request no sugar syrup, substitute lemon juice for flavouring, and avoid high-sugar fruit toppings such as longan and lychee to limit postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~5.8 g/100 g from coconut milk or coconut-based toppings, combined with 9.2 g/100 g added sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or omit coconut-based toppings to reduce saturated fat intake, and request no sugar syrup to lower the atherogenic sugar load; the aiyu jelly base itself is low in saturated fat and sodium and is acceptable in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aiyu jelly with toppings is a popular Singaporean and Taiwanese cold dessert made from the seeds of Ficus pumila var. awkeotsang (aiyu fig). The jelly is formed by rubbing the dried seeds in cold water, releasing natural pectin that sets into a translucent, soft, and mildly flavoured gel without any heat. The base jelly has a delicate, slightly floral taste and a smooth, silky texture. It is typically served in a bowl with assorted toppings that may include grass jelly (xiancao), tapioca pearls, fresh or canned fruit such as longan, lychee, or grass jelly cubes, and sweetened with sugar syrup or fresh lemon juice. The dish is refreshing and low in sodium (9 mg/100 g) but contains moderate sugar (9.2 g/100 g) and significant fat (6.79 g/100 g) primarily from coconut-based toppings. The estimated saturated fat of approximately 5.8 g/100 g from coconut milk toppings raises concern for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. Dietary fibre is low at 1.2 g/100 g. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low-medium), driven upward by sugar syrup and tapioca pearls. Clinically, this dessert is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification — specifically, omitting sugar syrup and coconut-based toppings. The aiyu jelly base itself is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and halal. It is commonly consumed as a snack or dessert in hawker centres and dessert stalls across Singapore and Taiwan.

What are the calories in aiyu jelly with toppings?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02030002, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from known coconut-based topping composition and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using tapioca and sweetened jelly composites, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (5.8 g/100 g) is estimated based on the known composition of coconut milk toppings commonly served with aiyu jelly; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. Monounsaturated fat (0.5 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) are estimated from the same coconut-topping composition basis. Trans fat is confirmed 0.0 g as no hydrogenated fats are used. 2. Potassium (40 mg/100 g) is estimated from HPB contextual data and USDA analogous fruit jelly entries; not directly reported by HPB for this item. 3. Cholesterol is 0.0 g — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dessert with no animal-derived ingredients in the base formulation. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dessert are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. 5. GI of 50 is an analogous composite estimate; no direct GI measurement for aiyu jelly with toppings exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for aiyu jelly with toppings as of 2024.
